narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 47% chance that you might not pass the bar exam on your first try, which is above our threshold for approval. This prediction draws from your academic record, like being in the 5th decile for Year 1 grades—slightly below the typical student's 6th decile position—and especially your 1st decile standing in Year 3, which is much lower than the average around the 5th or 6th decile. Your LSAT score of 29.5 also played a significant role, as it's notably below the dataset average of about 37 that we see among students who typically pass. Coming from an upper-middle family income bracket, which is around average, along with your Hispanic background—less common in the dataset where most are White—further influenced the model's view toward a higher risk of struggling on the bar. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.1 is just a touch under the 3.2 average, your choice to study full-time like most applicants, and being female in a group that's about evenly split, all nudged the prediction in the same direction, though with less weight. The strongest push toward predicting failure came from your low Year 3 decile ranking, followed closely by your Year 1 decile, then your LSAT score, family income, racial background, undergrad GPA, full-time status, and gender. Overall, these factors made your profile look riskier for bar success compared to typical admitted students, who tend to have stronger mid-to-high deciles, higher LSATs, and more common demographic matches. While some elements like your background can't be changed, you have real opportunities to shift the odds. Focus on boosting your academic deciles through dedicated study habits or extra courses if reapplying after more undergrad work. Retaking the LSAT to aim closer to or above 37 could dramatically improve future predictions, as it's one of the top influencers. Building a stronger GPA trajectory would also help counterbalance those lower deciles. With targeted prep, many students in similar spots turn things around and pass the bar confidently—keep pushing, and consider consulting advisors for personalized bar study plans. Your full-time commitment is already a plus, so lean into that strength while addressing the academics.
